current_fact.fact_text: On May 13, 1995, Alison Hargreaves stood on the summit of Everest without oxygen or Sherpa support, just months before she would die on K2 in another uncompromising climb. Her story embodies both the luminous bravery and the brutal cost of pushing human limits in the so‑called ‘death zone.’

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

current_fact.match_reason: A climber fully surrendering to extreme altitude parallels an episode about immersion—going below the surface into altered states, whether underwater or high above the clouds.

published.fact_text: In 1861, Australian astronomer John Tebbutt spotted a ghostly streak in the sky from his backyard observatory in Windsor, New South Wales: the Great Comet of 1861. It grew so bright that, at its peak, observers reported seeing the comet’s tail stretching across a huge portion of the night sky, briefly turning Earth’s skies into a cosmic light show.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A spectacular comet discovery pairs naturally with an episode about reimagining empty space as potential, echoing the cosmic, expansive mood of ‘Space Shift’.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1958, adventurer Ben Carlin completed a ten‑year odyssey to become the first and only person to circumnavigate the globe in an amphibious vehicle. His modified World War II army jeep, named Half‑Safe, rumbled over continents and bobbed across oceans for nearly 79,000 kilometers in total.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A wild, decade‑long road-and-sea trip perfectly matches an upbeat travel‑themed mix built for motion and new beginnings.

published.fact_text: In 1995, British mountaineer Alison Hargreaves reached the summit of Mount Everest without supplemental oxygen or Sherpa support, a feat so extreme that even many elite climbers won’t attempt it. She did it as a 33‑year‑old mother of two, carrying a homemade cloth doll her daughter had given her for luck.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both stories follow a single determined human into harsh, high‑risk environments—Everest’s death zone and Arctic seas—wrapped in reflective, cinematic soundscapes.

published.fact_text: On May 13, 1912, the Royal Flying Corps was established in the United Kingdom as a pioneering military aviation arm, long before air power became central to warfare. Its early pilots flew fragile, open‑cockpit biplanes made of wood and fabric, laying the foundations for what would become the Royal Air Force.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: The birth of military flight evokes soaring over rugged landscapes; an episode inspired by mountains and open spaces mirrors that sense of altitude and airy perspective.

published.fact_text: In 1862, enslaved pilot Robert Smalls secretly commandeered the Confederate steamboat Planter in Charleston Harbor, sailed it past coastal defenses using the correct signal codes, and delivered it to the U.S. Navy. His daring escape not only freed himself and his family but made him the first Black man to command a United States ship.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: Smalls’s act of liberation aligns with Afrobeat’s roots in resistance and freedom struggles, making an episode about African musical revolution a fitting soundtrack.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1501, Italian navigator Amerigo Vespucci set sail under the Portuguese flag toward lands that would eventually bear his name. His vivid letters describing a “New World” helped convince Europeans that these territories were not Asia’s outskirts, but an entirely separate pair of continents.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: Vespucci’s world‑reframing voyage pairs with an episode about travel as a way to shift perspective, both treating journeys as quiet meditations that change how we see the map.

published.fact_text: In 1998, India conducted two more underground nuclear tests at Pokhran, capping a five‑test series that announced its nuclear weapons capability to the world. The blasts, code‑named Shakti, triggered international sanctions and a seismic wave that was literally felt across continents by monitoring stations.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: An episode titled “November Bomb” with explosive, high‑energy tracks mirrors the literal and geopolitical shockwaves of a major nuclear test series.

published.fact_text: On May 13, 1996, a ferocious storm system swept through Bangladesh, unleashing a tornado and severe thunderstorms that killed around 600 people. In minutes, entire villages were flattened, highlighting how vulnerable densely populated, low‑lying regions are to extreme weather.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A devastating storm connects thematically to an episode built around rain, weather, and the hope that even the harshest downpours eventually pass.

published.fact_text: In 1980, an F3 tornado carved through Kalamazoo County, Michigan, tearing roofs from buildings, flipping cars, and killing five people in just a few minutes. The disaster prompted a federal emergency declaration and became a textbook case for studying tornado intensity on the Fujita scale.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The sudden, violent energy of a tornado is mirrored by a high‑voltage rock set, like an atmospheric ‘electric shock’ translated into guitar and drums.

published.fact_text: On this day in 2014, an underground coal mine in Soma, Turkey, experienced an explosion and fire that killed 301 miners, making it the deadliest mining disaster in Turkish history. The tragedy exposed deep issues in mine safety standards and sparked nationwide protests and mourning.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A catastrophic industrial failure invites reflection on how societies learn (or fail to learn) from tragedy, resonating with an episode about the deep, sometimes painful process of learning and change.

published.fact_text: In 2012, Mexican authorities discovered 49 dismembered bodies dumped beside Federal Highway 40 near Cadereyta Jiménez, a brutal cartel massacre intended as a public message. The chilling scene underscored how highways—meant to connect cities—had become stages for criminal terror.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A grim episode of cartel violence in Mexico is paired with Residente’s raw, politically charged Latin music, which often confronts the realities of violence and power in the Spanish‑speaking world.

published.fact_text: On May 13, 1990, a football match in Zagreb between Dinamo and Red Star Belgrade erupted into a massive riot between rival ultras, the Bad Blue Boys and the Delije. The clashes on the pitch and in the stands are often seen as a symbolic prelude to the violent breakup of Yugoslavia.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A stadium riot charged with political tension fits the explosive, protest‑driven energy of Rage Against The Machine, where music and unrest collide.

published.fact_text: In 1958, during a visit to Caracas, Venezuela, U.S. vice president Richard Nixon’s motorcade was attacked by an angry crowd that rocked, kicked, and spat on his car. The near‑lynching unfolded so fast that Secret Service agents later said they believed they might be witnessing a sitting vice president’s assassination in real time.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A tense diplomatic journey gone wrong fits with a sprawling, multi‑stop musical ‘destination’ mix, where movement between places and moods is central.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1958, amid the Algerian War of Independence, a group of French military officers in Algiers launched a coup demanding a government of national unity led by Charles de Gaulle. The May 1958 crisis would ultimately topple the Fourth Republic and usher in a new constitution and the Fifth Republic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #29 - Berber France Meets Arabic Italy
episode.description: Yasmin takes us back to her university years, where she met Abdel - an Algerian-French friend with a big heart and endless optimism. Their friendship grew around music, identity, and playful arguments about whether a song was originally Italian or French. Music that exists in both versions, alongside stories of finding a home, boxing training, and car rides filled with music.

published.match_reason: A French–Algerian political crisis connects neatly with a Franco‑Maghrebi–Italian episode about identity, migration, and cultural crossings between France and North Africa.

published.fact_text: In 1940, as German forces crossed the Meuse River and began their swift invasion of France, Winston Churchill addressed the House of Commons with his famous “blood, toil, tears, and sweat” speech. It was his first speech as prime minister, rallying a nation facing one of its darkest hours.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

published.match_reason: Churchill’s wartime resolve and stirring oratory pair with an introspective British‑Isles episode about weathering transitions and finding what grounds you when the world shifts.

published.fact_text: On May 13, 1943, the Axis forces in Tunisia surrendered after Operations Vulcan and Strike, ending the North African campaign of World War II. In a matter of days, over 200,000 Axis troops laid down their arms, a defeat so large it was compared to Stalingrad.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: The end of a North African desert campaign is sonically matched with Tuareg desert blues—music literally shaped by the sands where armies once clashed.

published.fact_text: In 1846, the United States formally declared war on Mexico after a border clash following the annexation of Texas, igniting the Mexican–American War. The conflict would redraw the map, ultimately transferring vast territories—including California, New Mexico, and Arizona—from Mexico to the U.S.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: A war that reshaped Latin North America and the U.S.–Mexico border pairs with an episode unpacking a modern Latin urban genre born from those intertwined geographies.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1779, the War of the Bavarian Succession ended with the Treaty of Teschen, negotiated with the help of French and Russian mediators. The conflict was so indecisive and casualty‑light that it became nicknamed the “Potato War,” as armies spent more time stealing food than fighting battles.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A largely bloodless ‘Potato War’ ending in negotiation rather than carnage suits the relaxed, sun‑soaked, almost anticlimactic peace of an ‘Endless Summer’ jazz episode.

published.fact_text: In 1110, Baldwin I of Jerusalem captured Beirut from the Fatimid Caliphate with the aid of a Genoese fleet, turning a thriving Mediterranean port into a Crusader stronghold. The siege showcased how maritime powers could tilt the balance of power along the Levantine coast.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A medieval maritime siege over a key Mediterranean port fits with an episode explicitly framed as a musical boat journey across those same waters.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1612, legendary swordsmen Miyamoto Musashi and Sasaki Kojiro fought their famous duel on Ganryū Island. Musashi arrived late, carved a wooden sword from an oar, and defeated Kojiro with a single crushing blow, cementing his reputation as Japan’s most enigmatic duelist.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: A mythic duel of blades pairs with the fierce, uncompromising energy of rock guitar heroes, translating sword‑fighting bravado into distorted riffs and defiant rhythm.

published.fact_text: In 2012, Mexican Federal Highway 40 became the grim site where 49 dismembered bodies were dumped as part of the Cadereyta Jiménez massacre, one of the most shocking cartel atrocities in recent memory. The killers even left banners claiming responsibility, weaponizing fear as much as bullets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #26 - Canserbero
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the story of Canserbero, the Venezuelan rapper who reshaped Latin American hip hop with his raw voice and deeply reflective lyrics. A journey into his music, his vision, and the legacy of an artist who transformed personal and social struggles into powerful storytelling.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como una película y su tragedia, comedia y ficción

published.match_reason: This brutal act of Latin American cartel violence is paired with Canserbero’s dark, socially conscious Latin rap, which often confronts death, injustice, and the psychology of violence.

published.fact_text: On May 13, 2014, an explosion and fire deep inside a coal mine in Soma, Turkey, rapidly consumed oxygen and released toxic gases, trapping workers underground. Rescue efforts lasted days, but 301 miners died, turning a quiet mining town into the center of international scrutiny and grief.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: The mine disaster’s trauma and the long aftermath of grief resonate with an episode about physical injury, slow recovery, and learning to move forward after catastrophe.

published.fact_text: In 1958, during the May crisis over Algeria, Ben Carlin quietly completed his amphibious circumnavigation while France grappled with a threatened coup in Algiers demanding a government led by de Gaulle. The Algiers putsch showed how a colonial war could destabilize politics at home and usher in an entirely new constitutional order.

episode.title: Morning Drops #02 - Waves of the Maghreb
episode.description: Oriental vibes guided by Yasmin. Discovering curious facts about the Maghreb, together with its fascinating music! A wonderful trip with a selection of songs from North Africa.
songs: Nass El Ghiwane - El Madi Fate

published.match_reason: A crisis centered on French Algeria naturally aligns with an episode dedicated to Maghreb sounds and stories, connecting political upheaval to the region’s cultural heartbeat.

published.fact_text: On May 13, 1995, Alison Hargreaves stood on the summit of Everest without oxygen or Sherpa support, just months before she would die on K2 in another uncompromising climb. Her story embodies both the luminous bravery and the brutal cost of pushing human limits in the so‑called ‘death zone.’

A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
